6	
7	# Rust Coding Guide (RCG) - Pattern Reference
8	
9	## Core Philosophy
10	
11	RCG is deliberately minimal. Trust Claude's inherent Rust idioms for error handling, struct design, documentation, and general style. This guide covers only project-specific conventions that override or extend standard Rust practice.
12	
13	The primary concern is **minting discipline** — ensuring all identifiers follow the project's prefix naming rules for clarity, grep-ability, and ease of discussion in non-Rust-aware contexts.
14	
15	## Crate Boilerplate
16	
17	Every Rust file that serves as a crate root must include these attributes. This includes `lib.rs`, `main.rs`, and every integration test file in `tests/` (each compiles as a separate crate).
18	
19	```rust
20	#![allow(non_camel_case_types)]
21	#![allow(private_interfaces)]
22	#![deny(warnings)]
23	```
24	
25	- `non_camel_case_types`: Allows prefixed type names like `jjrg_Gallops`
26	- `private_interfaces`: Allows z-prefixed (internal) types in public enum variants
27	- `deny(warnings)`: All warnings are build failures — no warning is acceptable in committed code
28	
29	The `allow` directives must appear after `deny(warnings)` to override it for RCG-specific naming patterns. The `deny` directive ensures warnings never accumulate — a warning today becomes a mystery tomorrow.
30	
31	## Import Discipline
32	
33	`use` blocks churn more than almost anything else in a file — every new symbol referenced adds an import, every removed reference drops one. Format them for **change-proportional diffs**: one item per line, alphabetically ordered.
34	
35	### Rule
36	
37	A braced `use` list with 2+ items places each item on its own line, in alphabetical order, with a trailing comma:
38	
39	```rust
40	// ✅ One per line, sorted — adding/removing a symbol is a single-line diff
41	// at a deterministic position
42	use crate::rbtdrm_manifest::{
43	    RBTDRM_COLOPHON_KLUDGE_BOTTLE,
44	    RBTDRM_COLOPHON_KLUDGE_SENTRY,
45	    RBTDRM_FIXTURE_KLUDGE_TADMOR,
46	};
47	
48	// ❌ Width-packed — adding one symbol reflows the line; two branches each
49	//    adding an import to the same packed line collide on merge
50	use crate::rbtdrm_manifest::{
51	    RBTDRM_COLOPHON_KLUDGE_BOTTLE, RBTDRM_COLOPHON_KLUDGE_SENTRY,
52	};
53	```
54	
55	### Rationale
56	
57	The diff of an import edit should be proportional to the change: one symbol added = one line added, never a reflow of neighbors. One-per-line keeps `git blame` precise (each import traces to the commit that introduced it) and removes the spurious merge conflicts width-packing creates. Alphabetical order makes the insertion point deterministic — there is no "where does this go" decision, and two branches adding different symbols sort to different positions, so they don't collide.
58	
59	### Note on rustfmt
60	
61	This project does not run `cargo fmt`; this discipline is review-enforced. Two of rustfmt's behaviors bear on it:
62	
63	- **Alphabetization** is rustfmt's stable default (`reorder_imports`, on by default) — consistent with this rule.
64	- **One-per-line** (`imports_layout = "Vertical"`) is **nightly-only**. On the stable toolchain, setting it in `rustfmt.toml` is rejected with a warning and silently ignored (measured: `Warning: can't set imports_layout = Vertical, unstable features are only available in nightly channel`). `max_width = 1` would force verticality but is a global sledgehammer — it maximally wraps *all* expressions, not just imports — so it is not a targeted substitute.
65	
66	Net: until a nightly `cargo fmt` is adopted (a larger decision — rustfmt has no imports-only mode and would reformat the whole codebase), one-per-line is held by review, alphabetization happens to match rustfmt's stable default.
67	
68	### Smell Test
69	
70	If a single `use` line carries more than one imported item, or the items are not in sorted order, split and sort.
71	
72	## File Naming
73	
74	### Source Files
75	
76	Pattern: `{cipher}r{classifier}_{name}.rs`
77	
78	| Component | Description |
79	|-----------|-------------|
80	| `{cipher}` | Project cipher (2-5 chars): `jj`, `vv`, `vo`, `cm`, `bu` |
81	| `r` | Rust source marker |
82	| `{classifier}` | Single letter, name typically starts with this letter |
83	| `{name}` | Descriptive snake_case name |
84	
85	Examples:
86	- `jjrg_gallops.rs` — JJ Rust, classifier `g`, "gallops"
87	- `jjrc_core.rs` — JJ Rust, classifier `c`, "core"
88	- `vvcg_guard.rs` — VV Core, classifier `g`, "guard"
89	
90	### Test Files
91	
92	Pattern: `{cipher}t{classifier}_{name}.rs`
93	
94	Tests use `t` instead of `r` after the cipher, with matching classifier:
95	
96	| Source | Test |
97	|--------|------|
98	| `jjrg_gallops.rs` | `jjtg_gallops.rs` |
99	| `jjrc_core.rs` | `jjtc_core.rs` |
100	| `vvcg_guard.rs` | `vvtg_guard.rs` |
101	
102	Module wiring in `lib.rs`:
103	```rust
104	pub mod jjrg_gallops;
105	
106	#[cfg(test)]
107	mod jjtg_gallops;
108	```
109	
110	## Declaration Naming
111	
112	**All public declarations from a source file must start with that file's full prefix.**
113	
114	This is non-negotiable. It enables grep-based discovery and clear provenance in discussions.
115	
116	### Naming Patterns by Type
117	
118	For a file with prefix `jjrg`:
119	
120	| Declaration | Public Pattern | Example | Internal Pattern | Example |
121	|-------------|---------------|---------|-----------------|---------|
122	| Struct | `{prefix}_PascalName` | `jjrg_Gallops` | `z{prefix}_PascalName` | `zjjrg_Output` |
123	| Enum | `{prefix}_PascalName` | `jjrg_PaceState` | `z{prefix}_PascalName` | `zjjrg_ParseMode` |
124	| Function | `{prefix}_snake_name` | `jjrg_load()` | `z{prefix}_snake_name` | `zjjrg_validate()` |
125	| Constant | `{PREFIX}_SCREAMING` | `JJRG_DEFAULT_PATH` | `Z{PREFIX}_SCREAMING` | `ZJJRG_MAX_DEPTH` |
126	| Trait | `{prefix}_TraitName` | `jjrg_Storable` | `z{prefix}_TraitName` | `zjjrg_Parseable` |
127	| Type alias | `{prefix}_AliasName` | `jjrg_Result` | `z{prefix}_AliasName` | `zjjrg_InternalResult` |
128	
129	### Impl Methods
130	
131	Methods on prefixed types also carry the prefix:
132	
133	```rust
134	impl jjrg_Gallops {
135	    pub fn jjrg_load(path: &Path) -> Result<Self, String> { ... }
136	    pub fn jjrg_save(&self, path: &Path) -> Result<(), String> { ... }
137	}
138	```
139	
140	Rationale: When reading `gallops.jjrg_save(path)`, the provenance is immediately clear. In discussions, "call jjrg_save" is unambiguous.
141	
142	## Internal Declarations
143	
144	**The `z`/`Z` prefix applies uniformly to all internal declaration types** — structs, enums, functions, constants, traits, and type aliases. The prefix letter follows the casing context: lowercase `z` for snake_case and PascalCase names, uppercase `Z` for SCREAMING_SNAKE constants. See the Internal Pattern columns in the naming table above.
145	
146	This parallels BCG's `z«prefix»_` convention for bash. The key difference: BCG also uses `z_«name»` for local variables because bash has flat function scope. Rust's block scoping eliminates this need — local variables use normal names without any z-prefix.
147	
148	Internal items use `pub(crate)` visibility to enable testing from separate test files:
149	
150	```rust
151	pub(crate) fn zjjrg_validate_silks(s: &str) -> bool {
152	    // Internal helper, testable from jjtg_gallops.rs
153	}
154	
155	pub(crate) const ZJJRG_MAX_DEPTH: usize = 16;
156	
157	pub(crate) struct zjjrg_Output {
158	    pub(crate) heat_silks: String,
159	}
160	```
161	
162	The `z`/`Z` prefix signals "do not call from outside this module's conceptual boundary" even though Rust visibility permits crate-internal access.
163	
164	### Visibility and Derive Macros
165	
166	When using derive macros like `clap`, internal argument structs (`z{prefix}_*Args`) may appear in public enum variants. Rust warns about this visibility mismatch. The `#![allow(private_interfaces)]` attribute in Crate Boilerplate suppresses this expected noise — the `z` prefix communicates intent to humans even when Rust forces technical exposure.
167	
168	### Struct Field Visibility for Tests
169	
170	When tests construct internal structs directly, both the struct AND its fields need `pub(crate)` visibility:
171	
172	```rust
173	// ❌ Tests can't construct this — fields are private
174	pub(crate) struct zjjrg_Output {
175	    heat_silks: String,
176	    pace_count: Option<u32>,
177	}
178	
179	// ✅ Tests can construct this — fields are accessible
180	pub(crate) struct zjjrg_Output {
181	    pub(crate) heat_silks: String,
182	    pub(crate) pace_count: Option<u32>,
183	}
184	```
185	
186	If tests only READ internal structs (returned by functions), field visibility doesn't matter. But if tests CONSTRUCT them for unit testing serialization, validation, or helper logic, fields must be `pub(crate)`.
187	
188	## Test Functions
189	
190	Test functions in `jjt{classifier}_{name}.rs` files use the test file's prefix:
191	
192	```rust
193	// In jjtg_gallops.rs
194	
195	use super::jjrg_gallops::*;
196	
197	#[test]
198	fn jjtg_load_empty_file() {
199	    // ...
200	}
201	
202	#[test]
203	fn jjtg_save_creates_parent_dirs() {
204	    // ...
205	}
206	```
207	
208	## Visibility Layering
209	
210	| Level | Visibility | Prefix | Testable |
211	|-------|------------|--------|----------|
212	| Public API | `pub` | `{prefix}_` | Yes |
213	| Internal | `pub(crate)` | `z{prefix}_` | Yes |
214	| Implementation detail | (none) | (rare) | No |
215	
216	Prefer `pub(crate)` with `z` prefix over true private for anything that benefits from testing. Reserve true private for trivial implementation details.
217	
218	## Minting Discipline
219	
220	All naming follows the project's minting rules. Key constraints:
221	
222	1. **Terminal exclusivity**: A prefix either IS a name or HAS children, never both
223	2. **Cipher ownership**: All identifiers derive from their project's cipher
224	3. **Grep-ability**: `grep jjrg_` finds all declarations from that module
225	
226	Reference: CLAUDE.md "Prefix Naming Discipline" section.
227	
228	## Interface Contamination Discipline
229	
230	**Interface contamination** is any change that expands the accepted input space, invocation forms, or alternative paths through an interface without explicit specification requirement.
231	
232	### Zeroes Theory
233	
234	Every tolerance, alias, fallback, or alternative path multiplies the enumerated state space of the project. The litmus test for any interface decision: **"How many zeroes got added to the enumerated state space because of this choice?"** If the answer isn't zero, the change requires explicit justification in the specification.
235	
236	### Rules
237	
238	1. **One canonical form**: Every function, parameter, command, and identifier has exactly one accepted form. Do not accept alternatives.
239	2. **No tolerances**: Do not add `strip_prefix`, case folding, alias acceptance, or alternative parse paths unless the specification explicitly requires temporary backwards compatibility.
240	3. **No undocumented defaults**: Do not add `unwrap_or`, `Option` with silent defaults, or fallback values that expand what callers can omit beyond what the interface contract specifies.
241	4. **No silent normalization**: Do not silently transform input to a canonical form. If input doesn't match the canonical form exactly, reject it.
242	5. **Documentation uses canonical form**: All documentation, examples, and generated content must use the single canonical form — never a tolerated alternative, even if the implementation happens to accept it.
243	
244	### Relationship to Other Disciplines
245	
246	- **String Boundary Discipline** is the serialization-specific instance: one const, exact match, no friendly parsing
247	- **Minting Discipline** is the naming-specific instance: one canonical name per terminal prefix
248	- This section is the general principle those specific disciplines instantiate
249	
250	### Smell Test
251	
252	If you can invoke the same operation two different ways (e.g., `"close"` and `"jjx_close"`), the interface is contaminated. If a function silently accepts `None` where the caller should always provide a value, the interface is contaminated. If documentation shows a shorter form than the canonical one, the documentation is contaminated.
253	
254	## String Boundary Discipline
255	
256	**Core principle**: This is an internal system. Strings that cross serialization boundaries (JSON, display, CLI) are produced and consumed by the same codebase. There are no external users typing variants.
257	
258	### Single Definition Rule
259	
260	Any string value that appears in serialization or display must have exactly **one** const definition. Both parse and emit reference that const.

# Rust Coding Guide (RCG) - Pattern Reference

## Core Philosophy

RCG is deliberately minimal. Trust Claude's inherent Rust idioms for error handling, struct design, documentation, and general style. This guide covers only project-specific conventions that override or extend standard Rust practice.

The primary concern is **minting discipline** — ensuring all identifiers follow the project's prefix naming rules for clarity, grep-ability, and ease of discussion in non-Rust-aware contexts.

## Crate Boilerplate

Every Rust file that serves as a crate root must include these attributes. This includes `lib.rs`, `main.rs`, and every integration test file in `tests/` (each compiles as a separate crate).

```rust
#![allow(non_camel_case_types)]
#![allow(private_interfaces)]
#![deny(warnings)]
```

- `non_camel_case_types`: Allows prefixed type names like `jjrg_Gallops`
- `private_interfaces`: Allows z-prefixed (internal) types in public enum variants
- `deny(warnings)`: All warnings are build failures — no warning is acceptable in committed code

The `allow` directives must appear after `deny(warnings)` to override it for RCG-specific naming patterns. The `deny` directive ensures warnings never accumulate — a warning today becomes a mystery tomorrow.

## Import Discipline

`use` blocks churn more than almost anything else in a file — every new symbol referenced adds an import, every removed reference drops one. Format them for **change-proportional diffs**: one item per line, alphabetically ordered.

### Rule

A braced `use` list with 2+ items places each item on its own line, in alphabetical order, with a trailing comma:

```rust
// ✅ One per line, sorted — adding/removing a symbol is a single-line diff
// at a deterministic position
use crate::rbtdrm_manifest::{
    RBTDRM_COLOPHON_KLUDGE_BOTTLE,
    RBTDRM_COLOPHON_KLUDGE_SENTRY,
    RBTDRM_FIXTURE_KLUDGE_TADMOR,
};

// ❌ Width-packed — adding one symbol reflows the line; two branches each
//    adding an import to the same packed line collide on merge
use crate::rbtdrm_manifest::{
    RBTDRM_COLOPHON_KLUDGE_BOTTLE, RBTDRM_COLOPHON_KLUDGE_SENTRY,
};
```

### Rationale

The diff of an import edit should be proportional to the change: one symbol added = one line added, never a reflow of neighbors. One-per-line keeps `git blame` precise (each import traces to the commit that introduced it) and removes the spurious merge conflicts width-packing creates. Alphabetical order makes the insertion point deterministic — there is no "where does this go" decision, and two branches adding different symbols sort to different positions, so they don't collide.

### Note on rustfmt

This project does not run `cargo fmt`; this discipline is review-enforced. Two of rustfmt's behaviors bear on it:

- **Alphabetization** is rustfmt's stable default (`reorder_imports`, on by default) — consistent with this rule.
- **One-per-line** (`imports_layout = "Vertical"`) is **nightly-only**. On the stable toolchain, setting it in `rustfmt.toml` is rejected with a warning and silently ignored (measured: `Warning: can't set imports_layout = Vertical, unstable features are only available in nightly channel`). `max_width = 1` would force verticality but is a global sledgehammer — it maximally wraps *all* expressions, not just imports — so it is not a targeted substitute.

Net: until a nightly `cargo fmt` is adopted (a larger decision — rustfmt has no imports-only mode and would reformat the whole codebase), one-per-line is held by review, alphabetization happens to match rustfmt's stable default.

### Smell Test

If a single `use` line carries more than one imported item, or the items are not in sorted order, split and sort.

## File Naming

### Source Files

Pattern: `{cipher}r{classifier}_{name}.rs`

| Component | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `{cipher}` | Project cipher (2-5 chars): `jj`, `vv`, `vo`, `cm`, `bu` |
| `r` | Rust source marker |
| `{classifier}` | Single letter, name typically starts with this letter |
| `{name}` | Descriptive snake_case name |

Examples:
- `jjrg_gallops.rs` — JJ Rust, classifier `g`, "gallops"
- `jjrc_core.rs` — JJ Rust, classifier `c`, "core"
- `vvcg_guard.rs` — VV Core, classifier `g`, "guard"

### Test Files

Pattern: `{cipher}t{classifier}_{name}.rs`

Tests use `t` instead of `r` after the cipher, with matching classifier:

| Source | Test |
|--------|------|
| `jjrg_gallops.rs` | `jjtg_gallops.rs` |
| `jjrc_core.rs` | `jjtc_core.rs` |
| `vvcg_guard.rs` | `vvtg_guard.rs` |

Module wiring in `lib.rs`:
```rust
pub mod jjrg_gallops;

#[cfg(test)]
mod jjtg_gallops;
```

## Declaration Naming

**All public declarations from a source file must start with that file's full prefix.**

This is non-negotiable. It enables grep-based discovery and clear provenance in discussions.

### Naming Patterns by Type

For a file with prefix `jjrg`:

| Declaration | Public Pattern | Example | Internal Pattern | Example |
|-------------|---------------|---------|-----------------|---------|
| Struct | `{prefix}_PascalName` | `jjrg_Gallops` | `z{prefix}_PascalName` | `zjjrg_Output` |
| Enum | `{prefix}_PascalName` | `jjrg_PaceState` | `z{prefix}_PascalName` | `zjjrg_ParseMode` |
| Function | `{prefix}_snake_name` | `jjrg_load()` | `z{prefix}_snake_name` | `zjjrg_validate()` |
| Constant | `{PREFIX}_SCREAMING` | `JJRG_DEFAULT_PATH` | `Z{PREFIX}_SCREAMING` | `ZJJRG_MAX_DEPTH` |
| Trait | `{prefix}_TraitName` | `jjrg_Storable` | `z{prefix}_TraitName` | `zjjrg_Parseable` |
| Type alias | `{prefix}_AliasName` | `jjrg_Result` | `z{prefix}_AliasName` | `zjjrg_InternalResult` |

### Impl Methods

Methods on prefixed types also carry the prefix:

```rust
impl jjrg_Gallops {
    pub fn jjrg_load(path: &Path) -> Result<Self, String> { ... }
    pub fn jjrg_save(&self, path: &Path) -> Result<(), String> { ... }
}
```

Rationale: When reading `gallops.jjrg_save(path)`, the provenance is immediately clear. In discussions, "call jjrg_save" is unambiguous.

## Internal Declarations

**The `z`/`Z` prefix applies uniformly to all internal declaration types** — structs, enums, functions, constants, traits, and type aliases. The prefix letter follows the casing context: lowercase `z` for snake_case and PascalCase names, uppercase `Z` for SCREAMING_SNAKE constants. See the Internal Pattern columns in the naming table above.

This parallels BCG's `z«prefix»_` convention for bash. The key difference: BCG also uses `z_«name»` for local variables because bash has flat function scope. Rust's block scoping eliminates this need — local variables use normal names without any z-prefix.

Internal items use `pub(crate)` visibility to enable testing from separate test files:

```rust
pub(crate) fn zjjrg_validate_silks(s: &str) -> bool {
    // Internal helper, testable from jjtg_gallops.rs
}

pub(crate) const ZJJRG_MAX_DEPTH: usize = 16;

pub(crate) struct zjjrg_Output {
    pub(crate) heat_silks: String,
}
```

The `z`/`Z` prefix signals "do not call from outside this module's conceptual boundary" even though Rust visibility permits crate-internal access.

### Visibility and Derive Macros

When using derive macros like `clap`, internal argument structs (`z{prefix}_*Args`) may appear in public enum variants. Rust warns about this visibility mismatch. The `#![allow(private_interfaces)]` attribute in Crate Boilerplate suppresses this expected noise — the `z` prefix communicates intent to humans even when Rust forces technical exposure.

### Struct Field Visibility for Tests

When tests construct internal structs directly, both the struct AND its fields need `pub(crate)` visibility:

```rust
// ❌ Tests can't construct this — fields are private
pub(crate) struct zjjrg_Output {
    heat_silks: String,
    pace_count: Option<u32>,
}

// ✅ Tests can construct this — fields are accessible
pub(crate) struct zjjrg_Output {
    pub(crate) heat_silks: String,
    pub(crate) pace_count: Option<u32>,
}
```

If tests only READ internal structs (returned by functions), field visibility doesn't matter. But if tests CONSTRUCT them for unit testing serialization, validation, or helper logic, fields must be `pub(crate)`.

## Test Functions

Test functions in `jjt{classifier}_{name}.rs` files use the test file's prefix:

```rust
// In jjtg_gallops.rs

use super::jjrg_gallops::*;

#[test]
fn jjtg_load_empty_file() {
    // ...
}

#[test]
fn jjtg_save_creates_parent_dirs() {
    // ...
}
```

## Visibility Layering

| Level | Visibility | Prefix | Testable |
|-------|------------|--------|----------|
| Public API | `pub` | `{prefix}_` | Yes |
| Internal | `pub(crate)` | `z{prefix}_` | Yes |
| Implementation detail | (none) | (rare) | No |

Prefer `pub(crate)` with `z` prefix over true private for anything that benefits from testing. Reserve true private for trivial implementation details.

## Minting Discipline

All naming follows the project's minting rules. Key constraints:

1. **Terminal exclusivity**: A prefix either IS a name or HAS children, never both
2. **Cipher ownership**: All identifiers derive from their project's cipher
3. **Grep-ability**: `grep jjrg_` finds all declarations from that module

Reference: CLAUDE.md "Prefix Naming Discipline" section.

## Interface Contamination Discipline

**Interface contamination** is any change that expands the accepted input space, invocation forms, or alternative paths through an interface without explicit specification requirement.

### Zeroes Theory

Every tolerance, alias, fallback, or alternative path multiplies the enumerated state space of the project. The litmus test for any interface decision: **"How many zeroes got added to the enumerated state space because of this choice?"** If the answer isn't zero, the change requires explicit justification in the specification.

### Rules

1. **One canonical form**: Every function, parameter, command, and identifier has exactly one accepted form. Do not accept alternatives.
2. **No tolerances**: Do not add `strip_prefix`, case folding, alias acceptance, or alternative parse paths unless the specification explicitly requires temporary backwards compatibility.
3. **No undocumented defaults**: Do not add `unwrap_or`, `Option` with silent defaults, or fallback values that expand what callers can omit beyond what the interface contract specifies.
4. **No silent normalization**: Do not silently transform input to a canonical form. If input doesn't match the canonical form exactly, reject it.
5. **Documentation uses canonical form**: All documentation, examples, and generated content must use the single canonical form — never a tolerated alternative, even if the implementation happens to accept it.

### Relationship to Other Disciplines

- **String Boundary Discipline** is the serialization-specific instance: one const, exact match, no friendly parsing
- **Minting Discipline** is the naming-specific instance: one canonical name per terminal prefix
- This section is the general principle those specific disciplines instantiate

### Smell Test

If you can invoke the same operation two different ways (e.g., `"close"` and `"jjx_close"`), the interface is contaminated. If a function silently accepts `None` where the caller should always provide a value, the interface is contaminated. If documentation shows a shorter form than the canonical one, the documentation is contaminated.

## String Boundary Discipline

**Core principle**: This is an internal system. Strings that cross serialization boundaries (JSON, display, CLI) are produced and consumed by the same codebase. There are no external users typing variants.

### Single Definition Rule

Any string value that appears in serialization or display must have exactly **one** const definition. Both parse and emit reference that const.

329	## Constant Discipline (Beyond Strings)
330	
331	String Boundary Discipline covers serialized strings. This section extends to **all** repeated values.
332	
333	### Rule
334	
335	Any magic value that appears 2+ times requires a const. This includes numbers, format strings, and computed values.
336	
337	### Derivation Rule
338	
339	If value B depends on value A, always derive B from A at the use site. Never define B as a separate constant.
340	
341	```rust
342	// ✅ Length derived from the const
343	pub const JJRG_UNKNOWN_COMMIT: &str = "0000000";
344	let short_arg = format!("--short={}", JJRG_UNKNOWN_COMMIT.len());
345	
346	fn zjjrg_is_commit_sha(s: &str) -> bool {
347	    s.len() == JJRG_UNKNOWN_COMMIT.len() && s.chars().all(|c| c.is_ascii_hexdigit())
348	}
349	
350	// ❌ Magic number exists separately — drift risk
351	const COMMIT_LEN: usize = 7;  // Could diverge from JJRG_UNKNOWN_COMMIT
352	format!("--short=7")          // Hardcoded, not derived
353	```
354	
355	### Identity Rule
356	
357	The const name must identify the specific value, not just its category. A category-only suffix is ambiguous and becomes a terminal exclusivity violation the moment a second value appears in the same category.
358	
359	```rust
360	// ✅ Suffix identifies the specific command
361	const JJRGS_CMD_NAME_GET_SPEC: &str = "jjx_get_spec";
362	const JJRLG_CMD_NAME_BIND: &str = "jjx_bind";
363	const JJRLG_CMD_NAME_SEND: &str = "jjx_send";
364	
365	// ❌ Suffix names the role, not the identity — deferred terminal exclusivity debt
366	const JJRGS_CMD_NAME: &str = "jjx_get_spec";
367	const JJRLG_CMD_NAME: &str = "jjx_bind";  // Blocks JJRLG_CMD_NAME_SEND
368	```
369	
370	### Smell Test
371	
372	If `grep '7'` (or any literal) finds the same value used for the same purpose in multiple places, extract a const and derive.
373	
374	## Constructor Discipline
375	
376	When a struct requires non-trivial initialization (timestamps, captured values, computed defaults), define a constructor helper. All creation sites use the helper.
377	
378	### Rationale
379	
380	Scattered struct construction leads to:
381	- Inconsistent initialization (some sites forget fields)
382	- Duplicated capture logic (timestamps, git SHAs)
383	- Drift when struct fields change
384	
385	### Pattern
386	
387	```rust
388	// ✅ Constructor centralizes initialization logic
389	pub fn jjrg_make_tack(
390	    state: jjrg_PaceState,
391	    text: String,
392	    silks: String,
393	    direction: Option<String>,
394	) -> jjrg_Tack {
395	    jjrg_Tack {
396	        ts: timestamp_full(),           // Captured once
397	        state,
398	        text,
399	        silks,
400	        commit: jjrg_capture_commit_sha(),  // Captured once
401	        direction,
402	    }
403	}
404	
405	// All creation sites use the helper
406	let tack = jjrg_make_tack(PaceState::Rough, text, silks, None);
407	```
408	
409	```rust
410	// ❌ Raw struct construction scattered across files
411	// In jjx_slate:
412	jjrg_Tack { ts: timestamp_full(), state, text, silks, commit: capture_sha(), direction }
413	// In jjx_tally:
414	jjrg_Tack { ts: timestamp_full(), state, text, silks, commit: capture_sha(), direction }
415	// In deserializer:
416	jjrg_Tack { ts, state, text, silks: migrated, commit: UNKNOWN.to_string(), direction }
417	```
418	
419	### When to Use
420	
421	- Struct has fields requiring runtime capture (timestamps, git state)
422	- Struct has fields with non-trivial defaults
423	- Struct is created in 2+ locations
424	
425	### Exception
426	
427	Test files may construct structs directly with test values (no runtime capture needed).
428	
429	## Comment Discipline
430	
431	Comments describe what code does permanently, not why it was written this session.
432	
433	### Prohibited
434	
435	- References to pace names, coronets, firemarks (`₢AEAAI`, `₣AB`)
436	- References to heat names or silks
437	- Phrases like "this migration", "for this pace", "current task"
438	- Explanations of what changed (that's what git commits are for)
439	
440	### Permitted
441	
442	- What the code does in general terms
443	- Why a non-obvious approach was chosen (permanent rationale)
444	- Constraints or invariants the code maintains
445	
446	```rust
447	// ✅ Permanent description
448	// Use unknown commit placeholder for legacy data without commit tracking
449	
450	// ❌ Task-focused — meaningless to future readers
451	// Migration: if tack lacks commit, use JJRG_UNKNOWN_COMMIT for ₢AEAAI
452	
453	// ✅ Permanent rationale
454	// Custom deserializer handles legacy format where silks lived on Pace
455	
456	// ❌ Task-focused
457	// Added in tack-struct-rust-migration pace to support old JSON
458	```
459	
460	## Output Discipline
461	
462	**Core rule**: Every crate must route all output through a declared output module. No naked `println!`, `eprintln!`, `print!`, or `eprint!` anywhere except inside that module's implementation.
463	
464	### Why This Exists
465	
466	Raw print statements scatter output decisions across the codebase. This creates problems ranging from cosmetic (inconsistent format) to fatal (MCP servers use stdout for JSON-RPC — a stray `println!` corrupts the transport). A single output module centralizes the decision of where output goes, what format it takes, and how to disable it.
467	
468	### Sentinel Comment
469	
470	Every file that produces output must carry a standardized import with a sentinel comment:
471	
472	```rust
473	// RCG output discipline: all emission via apcrl_*! — no direct println!/eprintln!
474	use apcd::{apcrl_info_now, apcrl_error_now, apcrl_fatal_now};
475	```
476	
477	The tag `RCG output discipline:` is grep-able. The comment restates the rule at the point where violations would occur. Future editors see the prohibition when they open the file, not buried in a spec.
478	
479	### Four Severity Levels
480	
481	Every output module must provide these four levels with consistent semantics:
482	
483	| Level | Semantics | Continues? | Rate |
484	|-------|-----------|------------|------|
485	| `trace` | Development debugging | Yes | High — may compile out in release |
486	| `info` | Operational milestones | Yes | Low — always present |
487	| `error` | Recoverable failure | Yes | Hopefully rare |
488	| `fatal` | Unrecoverable | No — exits process | Terminal |

624	## Load-Bearing Complexity
625	
626	Every element in a system must carry weight — its removal would create a gap between intent and behavior. When similar things differ, the difference must be load-bearing or the code should be homogenized.
627	
628	Rust's type system enforces much of this mechanically: unused fields warn, unused variants warn, dead code warns. The disciplines above — Interface Contamination (no unearned input forms), Specification Authority (no unearned features), Constant Discipline (no unearned literals), Constructor Discipline (no unearned construction sites) — are each instantiations of this principle applied to specific domains. When evaluating a new pattern or extraction, ask: "Is this element load-bearing?" If not, it doesn't belong.
629	
630	## What RCG Does Not Cover
631	
632	Trust Claude's Rust idioms for:
633	
634	- Error handling (`Result<T, E>` patterns)
635	- Documentation (`///` doc comments)
636	- Module organization beyond naming
637	- Struct field design
638	- Lifetime annotations
639	- Generic patterns
640	- Async patterns
641	- Macro design
642	
643	## Quick Reference
644	
645	```rust
646	#![allow(non_camel_case_types)]
647	
648	// Public constants
649	pub const JJRG_DEFAULT_PATH: &str = ".claude/jjm/jjg_gallops.json";
650	
651	// Internal constants (z/Z prefix + pub(crate))
652	pub(crate) const ZJJRG_MAX_DEPTH: usize = 16;
653	
654	// Public types
655	pub struct jjrg_Gallops { ... }
656	pub enum jjrg_PaceState { ... }
657	
658	// Internal types (z prefix + pub(crate))
659	pub(crate) struct zjjrg_Output { ... }
660	
661	// Public functions
662	pub fn jjrg_load(path: &Path) -> Result<jjrg_Gallops, String> { ... }
663	
664	// Internal functions (z prefix + pub(crate))
665	pub(crate) fn zjjrg_validate(s: &str) -> bool { ... }
666	
667	// Impl methods - also prefixed
668	impl jjrg_Gallops {
669	    pub fn jjrg_new() -> Self { ... }
670	    pub fn jjrg_save(&self, path: &Path) -> Result<(), String> { ... }
671	    pub(crate) fn zjjrg_validate_internal(&self) -> bool { ... }
672	}
673	```
674	
675	## File Templates
676	
677	### Proprietary License Header
678	
679	```rust
680	// Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
681	// All rights reserved.
682	// SPDX-License-Identifier: LicenseRef-Proprietary
683	```

content.text:
RCG audit done — it's minimal (defers to Rust idioms; the project-specific bits are import discipline, prefix naming, comment discipline, load-bearing). My additions check out on most:

- **Import** (one-per-line, sorted within the colophon group, trailing comma) ✓
- **Naming** — `rbtdrk_*` private fns matching the sibling invest cases (the `case!` macro surfaces the fn name as the case label, so the non-`z` prefix is the file's deliberate convention) ✓
- **Load-bearing** — `rbtdrk_role_divest_impl` shared by both case fns, exactly parallel to `rbtdrk_role_invest_impl` ✓
- **No new constants / no output / no contamination** ✓

But **Comment Discipline** (RCG:429) catches two things I wrote — comments must describe what the code does permanently, **not what changed**:
1. The case-list comment narrates history: *"the same edges the per-cycle pre-divest used to surface before it moved out of `rbtdrk_role_invest_impl`"* — that's a changelog, prohibited.
2. Both comments carry `(H1)` — memo jargon that doesn't describe the code; the behavioral text already stands alone.

Fixing both:

content.text:
All green:
- **RCG fixes applied** (comment discipline — removed the changelog narration and the `(H1)` jargon).
- **Build clean** (exit 0) — re-added imports used, new fns referenced, no warnings under `deny(warnings)`.
- **137 unit tests pass** — including `rbtdtk_cases_registered`, so the case-count change (3→5) didn't break any registration assertion.

## What's now in place
`RBTDRK_CASES_CANONICAL_INVEST` (shared by **skirmish, dogfight, blockade**) is now:
```
governor_mantle → director_divest → retriever_divest → retriever_invest → director_invest
```
Every run of those three suites now does a full **teardown → reinvest** cycle: `rbgg_divest_*` revoke-before-delete + `poll_until_gone` debounce, then the invest **create** branch against a durably-gone SA — deliberately hammering the delete→recreate eventual-consistency edge each run. New `rbtdrk_role_divest_impl` + two case fns, mirroring the invest cases.

This is **uncommitted** and **beyond the notched pace ₢BYAAA** — it's test-infrastructure that adds the H1/eventual-consistency coverage. Flag: it could fold into ₢BYAAA (it validates that fix) or stand as its own pace. Your call on commit.

## Exercising it
The most targeted live exercise of the new path — no cloud build, no container fixtures, just the credential cycle:

```
tt/rbw-tf.FixtureRun.sh canonical-invest
```

That runs exactly the 5 cases above against the standing depot: divest (deletes canest-dir/canest-ret, runs my revoke) → reinvest (recreates them). It both exercises H1 and stresses the race the operator wants shaken out. (Full `dogfight` would do the same cycle plus the ~330s Cloud Build.)

Want me to run `canonical-invest` now? And after it's green, I can re-run the gcloud read-back to confirm the SAs came back clean with one key each — and check the project policy for any `deleted:` ghost from the divest.

content.text:
The live test earned its keep — it caught a **real bug in my revoke code**. Here's the failure:

```
rbgg_divest_director: Revoke Director IAM bindings before delete
  Token mint: attempt 1/2 hit SA propagation race (Invalid JWT Signature) — retried, recovered
  ERROR: Get repo IAM policy (HTTP 403): Permission 'artifactregistry.repositories.getIamPolicy'
         denied on …/repositories/cancbhm-canest3bhm100001-gar
```

## Root cause — I dropped Class C, which *does* fire on revoke
This is the **Class C "caller-recently-empowered"** race. `governor_mantle` runs immediately before the divest and re-mantles the **shared governor**, so the governor's `roles/owner` hasn't yet propagated to the **AR resource-scope** IAM cache → 403 on `getIamPolicy`. (The "Invalid JWT Signature" retries just before it confirm the governor's *key* was also fresh.)

When I made revoke "lean," I argued the propagation envelope was non-load-bearing because *the member being revoked is long-established*. That's true for **Class A/B (member-visibility)** — those genuinely can't fire on a revoke. But I wrongly swept up **Class C**, which is about the **caller** (the governor), not the member. The grant path carries Class C tolerance on its resource-scope sites for exactly this; revoke needs it too. My RBSCIG revoke contract even asserts "the governor's `roles/owner` reached every cache long ago" — that assumption is false whenever the governor is freshly mantled.

**This is real, not just a test artifact:** any governor re-mantle → divest hits it (the test does it every run; production hits it on governor key-rotation/recovery followed by a teardown).

## Proposed repair (precise, load-bearing)
- **`rbgi_revoke_repo_member` + `rbgi_revoke_sa_member`** (resource-scope): restore the propagation-retry envelope with **Class C only** (`403`, empty body-glob, time-bounded against `RBGC_PROPAGATION_DEADLINE_SEC`) — mirroring the grant path's resource-scope 403 tolerance.
- **`rbgi_revoke_project_member`** stays lean — project-scope absorbs governor empowerment instantly (the grant project-scope sites carry no 403 tolerance either).
- Keep **Class A/B dropped** — they provably can't fire on revoke.
- Correct the **RBSCIG** revoke contract + the function comments accordingly (Class C is load-bearing; A/B are not).

I'm surfacing rather than just fixing because this partially reverses the lean-revoke decision we worked through together, and it's security-sensitive credential code. I'm confident in the diagnosis and the Class-C-only scope — but the conservative alternative is to faithfully mirror the grant's full `400/400/403` on the resource-scope revokes. I'd take **Class-C-only** (load-bearing precise). Your call before I touch it.

**One side effect to note:** the fatal revoke died mid-way, so `director-canest-dir` currently has its **project + Mason-SA bindings revoked, repo binding intact, SA not deleted** — a partial teardown. A `rbw-adI canest-dir` re-invest restores it (and the Class C fix prevents the recurrence). Want me to restore it now, or leave it until the fix lands and the rerun recreates it?

lastPrompt: notch and run it.

content.content:
1	<!--
2	Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
3	SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0
4	See LICENSE for terms.
5	-->
6	
7	# Bash Console Guide (BCG) - Pattern Reference
8	
9	## Core Philosophy
10	
11	This pattern emerged from environments where hidden failures cascade into production outages. When bash scripts manage critical infrastructure, every potential failure point needs explicit handling at its location, making post-mortem analysis straightforward.
12	
13	The aesthetic of elegant bash pipelines trades debuggability for brevity. A failing command in `$(echo | sed | awk)` leaves no trace. Temp files make each transformation step visible, testable, and recoverable.
14	
15	Traps and signal handlers create action-at-a-distance effects that obscure control flow. When error handling lives at the point of failure rather than in distant handlers, code review and maintenance become predictable.
16	
17	Enterprise bash isn't about looking clever; it's about being boringly, reliably correct. These patterns optimize for the 3am debugging session, not the initial writing experience. Each external process spawn is a potential failure point, so bash builtins are preferred despite verbosity.
18	
19	The patterns reject bash 4+ features to maintain compatibility with older enterprise systems still running bash 3.2, particularly older RHEL/CentOS or macOS deployments.
20	
21	### Load-Bearing Complexity
22	
23	Every element in a system — every function extraction, every pattern variant, every structural distinction — must carry weight. An element is **load-bearing** when its removal would create a gap between intent and behavior. When similar things differ, ask whether the difference is load-bearing: if yes, document why; if no, homogenize.
24	
25	This is the general decision criterion that tools like Zeroes Theory instantiate. Zeroes Theory asks "how many zeroes did this add to state space?" — load-bearing complexity asks the prior question: "does this element earn its existence at all?"
26	
27	Concrete examples from this codebase:
28	
29	- **Four separate `rbgi_add_*_iam` functions** look like copy-paste, but each wraps a different GCP API with different failure modes. The variation is load-bearing — extracting a "generic IAM helper" would hide which API failed at 3am.
30	- **Three copy-pasted Secret Manager IAM blocks** are NOT load-bearing variation — same API, same failure class, same recovery. Extract a helper.
31	- **Not adding three-part structure to a simple `api_enable` spec definition** — the structure would add ceremony without carrying meaning. Non-load-bearing elements increase cognitive cost without increasing correctness.
32	
33	Non-load-bearing elements are the silent budget overruns of a codebase. Each one is small; their aggregate is what makes systems incomprehensible.
34	
35	## Module Architecture
36	
37	Every module has an implementation file. CLI entry points are only present if module requires standalone execution.
38	
39	```bash
40	«prefix»_«name».sh (implementation - REQUIRED, prefer single lowercase word, allow snake_case)
41	«prefix»_cli.sh (entry point - OPTIONAL, omit for library/utility modules)
42	```
43	
44	### CLI as Module Gateway
45	
46	**Rule**: Code outside the BCG module system must never call `z*_kindle()` directly. All external access to BCG modules goes through CLI scripts.
47	
48	**Pattern**:
49	```
50	External Code → CLI script → furnish() kindles → module functions
51	```
52	
53	**Why this matters**:
54	- Kindle sequences are implementation details that may change
55	- The CLI's `furnish()` owns the kindle graph (which modules to kindle, in what order)
56	- External code remains stable when module dependencies change
57	- CLIs provide the documented, stable API for module functionality
58	
59	**Anti-pattern**:
60	```bash
61	# ❌ External script kindling directly
62	source "${TOOLS_DIR}/rbob_bottle.sh"
63	zrbob_kindle                          # VIOLATION: kindle leaked outside CLI
64	rbob_charge
65	```
66	
67	**Correct pattern**:
68	```bash
69	# ✅ External script delegates to CLI
70	exec "${TOOLS_DIR}/rbob_cli.sh" rbob_charge "$@"
71	```
72	
73	**What counts as "external code"**:
74	- Entry point scripts (workbenches, testbenches, coordinators)
75	- TabTargets and launchers
76	- Any dispatcher or router that invokes module functionality
77	- Scripts that are not themselves BCG modules
78	
79	**What can call kindle**:
80	- A module's own CLI (in `furnish()`)
81	- Another CLI's `furnish()` (when CLI A depends on module B, A's furnish kindles B)
82	- Test harnesses that explicitly isolate module behavior
83	
84	**Filesystem marker**: The `_cli.sh` suffix and the executable bit must agree. Within a BCG module directory:
85	
86	- Every `*_cli.sh` MUST be executable
87	- Every other `*.sh` MUST NOT be executable
88	
89	This makes the gateway role visible to `ls -l`: a workbench, testbench, or implementation module carrying `+x` is a bug — direct execution bypasses the CLI's `furnish()` and the kindle graph it owns. Tabtargets in `tt/` and shipped tool binaries are not BCG modules and stand outside this rule.
90	
91	### Module-CLI Prefix Unity
92	
93	**Convention**: A module and its CLI share a prefix (`rbrn_regime.sh` + `rbrn_cli.sh`).
94	
95	When commands within a single CLI have different dependency weights (e.g., `validate` needs
96	only the regime file, `audit` needs GCP/OAuth), use **differential furnish**: the furnish
97	function receives the command name as `$1` and conditionally sources/kindles heavy
98	dependencies based on which command is being invoked.
99	
100	### Regime Module Archetype
101	
102	A **regime** is a sourced `.env` file containing typed configuration fields — the contract between human-authored configuration and the modules that consume it. When a module's purpose is validating a regime, it follows the regime archetype:
103	
104	| Component | Location | Purpose |
105	|-----------|----------|---------|
106	| `buv_*_enroll` calls | kindle | Declare field contracts (type, range, description) |
107	| `buv_scope_sentinel` | kindle (last, before KINDLED) | Catch undeclared `PREFIX_*` variables |
108	| `z«prefix»_enforce` | regime module | `buv_vet SCOPE` + custom format checks |
109	| `readonly` lock | after enforce | Lock enrolled variables against mutation |
110	| `*_validate` command | CLI | `buv_report` — explained per-field display |
111	| `*_render` command | CLI | `buv_render` — diagnostic dump |
112	
113	Furnish sequence: kindle → enforce → lock (ironclad gate before any command runs). After enforce succeeds, lock all enrolled `PREFIX_*` variables with `readonly` to prevent accidental mutation by downstream code. Any derived state (e.g., docker env arrays) must be built from validated values after enforce, before or during the lock step.
114	
115	**Singleton** regimes source config unconditionally in furnish. **Manifold** regimes (multiple instances, e.g. nameplates) source conditionally when a folio identifies the instance.
116	
117	Enrollment types and gating API: see `buv_validation.sh`.
118	
119	---
120	
121	## Function Patterns
122	
123	### Boilerplate Functions (one per module/CLI)
124	
125	| Function             | Location       | First Line                                         | Can Source?       | Can use buc_step? | Purpose                                          |
126	|----------------------|----------------|----------------------------------------------------|-------------------|-------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
127	| `z«prefix»_kindle`   | Implementation | `test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" \|\| buc_die`    | Yes (credentials) | No (use buc_log_*)| Define all kindle constants, set module state    |
128	| `z«prefix»_sentinel` | Implementation | `test "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" = "1" \|\| buc_die` | No                | No                | Guard all other functions                        |
129	| `z«prefix»_enforce`  | Implementation | `z«prefix»_sentinel`                               | No                | No                | Validate sourced config (regime archetype only)  |
130	| `z«prefix»_furnish`  | CLI only       | `buc_doc_env...`                                   | Yes (all deps)    | No                | Document env vars, source all deps, kindle       |
131	| Module header        | Implementation | `test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED:-}" \|\| buc_die`    | No                | N/A               | Prevent multiple inclusion                       |
132	| CLI header           | CLI            | `set -euo pipefail`                                | Yes (buc_command)  | N/A               | Source buc_command.sh only                       |
133	
134	**Kindle constant**: Any variable — internal (`Z«PREFIX»_SCREAMING_NAME`) or public (`«PREFIX»_SCREAMING_NAME`) — defined exclusively within the kindle function with `readonly`. No other function — including enroll, setup, or helper functions — may assign to kindle constants. This ensures module state is fully determined at kindle time and visible in one place.
135	
136	**Mutable kindle state**: A variable initialized in kindle but intentionally mutated after kindle returns (counters, accumulators, registry rolls, builder state). Uses **lowercase** `z_«prefix»_name` to visually distinguish from `readonly` kindle constants. Never apply `readonly` to mutable kindle state.
137	
138	**Tinder constant**: A public variable (`«PREFIX»_lower_name`) defined at module top level, immediately after the `Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED=1` guard. Tinder constants must resolve to pure string literals at source time — **no runtime dependency, no `$(…)` computation, no expansion of kindle constants or runtime variables**. Tinder-on-tinder expansion is permitted: a tinder value may interpolate `${«PREFIX»_lower_name}` for any tinder constant defined earlier in the same module's tinder block, since that expansion resolves at source time and preserves the no-runtime-dependency invariant. They are available immediately after sourcing — no kindle required. Use `SCREAMING` case for the prefix (module identity) and `lower_snake` case for the name to visually distinguish from kindle constants. This enables sourcing chains where a tinder constant from module A provides the path for sourcing module B's config in the furnish function. The name reflects their role in the kindle metaphor: tinder is the fixed material prepared before kindling — inert, unchanging, and enabling the fire to start.
139	
140	**KINDLED must be last**: `readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1` must be the final statement in kindle. Since sentinel checks this variable, setting it last guarantees all kindle constants, roll arrays, and enroll calls are complete before the module is considered operational. Any function calling sentinel before kindle finishes will correctly fail. The `readonly` makes re-kindling a loud error.
141	
142	### Regular Functions (output via printouts/temp files)
143	
144	This table defines scope: «prefix»_* is public, z«prefix»_* is internal.
145	
146	| Type            | Pattern              | First Line           | Parameters | buc_step | Documentation        |
147	|-----------------|----------------------|----------------------|------------|----------|----------------------|
148	| Public          | `«prefix»_«command»` | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | `"${1:-}"` | Yes      | Required (buc_doc_*) |
149	| Internal helper | `z«prefix»_«name»`   | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | `"${1:-}"` | Yes      | Recommended (buc_doc_*) |
150	
151	---
152	
153	## File Templates
154	
155	### Template 1: Shebang and Copyright
156	
157	```bash
158	#!/bin/bash
159	#
160	# Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
161	#
162	# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
163	# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
164	# You may obtain a copy of the License at
165	#
166	#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
167	#
168	# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
169	# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
170	# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
171	# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
172	# limitations under the License.
173	#
174	# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
175	#
176	```
177	
178	**Cross-module references need no per-file directive.** Files that reference cross-module `Z*_/RB*_` readonly state trip SC2153 (possible misspelling) and SC2154 (referenced but not assigned). Both are suppressed globally in `busc_shellcheckrc`, applied on the canonical lint path (the project's shellcheck tabtarget always passes `--rcfile`). No inline directive is carried — see **Shellcheck Integration** and **Prohibited Constructs § Inline Shellcheck Directives**.
179	
180	The `set -u` clause of `set -euo pipefail` is the runtime backstop for the same typo class SC2153 catches statically: a misspelled cross-module reference dies at first reference rather than silently expanding to empty.
181	
182	### Tabtarget Path Indirection
183	
184	A tabtarget in `tt/` is a colophon, not a program — it names *what* to run, never *how* to reach it. A single trampoline, `tt/z-launcher.sh`, owns the "how." Every other tabtarget is a declarative stub: a shebang, a `BURD_*` config block, and a byte-identical `exec` into the trampoline.
185	
186	```bash
187	#!/bin/bash
188	export BURD_LAUNCHER=launcher.rbw_workbench.sh
189	exec "${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}/z-launcher.sh" "${0##*/}" "${@}"
190	```
191	
192	The exec line carries no launcher token — it is identical, character for character, across every tabtarget. All per-tabtarget variation lives in the `BURD_*` block between the shebang and the exec: which launcher (`BURD_LAUNCHER`, always the first config line), and any dispatch flags (`BURD_NO_LOG`, `BURD_INTERACTIVE`) that follow.
193	
194	The trampoline has exactly two responsibilities:
195	
196	1. **Resolve the moorings location.** The consumer's configuration tree (launchers, regimes) lives under a moorings directory whose path the trampoline holds as a single hardcoded literal. This is the single point of customization at the tabtarget layer — the parallel to BURC at the CLI layer. Move the moorings, edit one line; every tabtarget follows.
197	2. **Normalize cwd to repo root.** The trampoline `cd`s to the repo root before dispatching, so every workbench starts from a deterministic directory regardless of where the user invoked the tabtarget. Downstream code relies on a known cwd rather than reconstructing one.
198	
199	**The launcher name.** `BURD_LAUNCHER` is a bare launcher basename — `launcher.<id>_workbench.sh`. Every launcher co-locates in the moorings launcher directory (`rbml_launchers/`), so the trampoline resolves the basename directly against that one directory and exec's it, forwarding the tabtarget basename and user args unchanged. `BURD_LAUNCHER` is not a private positional convention: it is a regime variable — declared in `burd_regime`, allowlisted in `bud_dispatch` — so the launcher identity travels the same validated `BURD_*` channel as every other dispatch value, and flows downstream unchanged.
200	
201	Why name the launcher in a config variable rather than pass it as a positional dispatch token? **Because the byte-identical exec line is load-bearing.** When the only thing that varies between two tabtargets is their `BURD_*` block, a tabtarget is purely declarative — it states *which launcher and which flags*, and holds no dispatch logic that can drift from the trampoline's expectations. The launcher identity rides the same validated `BURD_*` channel as every other dispatch value rather than a parallel argument slot, and the exec collapses to a true constant the qualifier checks against a single literal.
202	
203	This indirection is the tabtarget-layer instance of CLI as Module Gateway: a tabtarget is external code, and it reaches module functionality only through a launcher the trampoline resolves — never by sourcing or kindling directly. The launcher → dispatch chain it hands off to is what provides the Dispatch-Provided Directory Variables described next.
204	
205	### Dispatch-Provided Directory Variables
206	
207	Three BURD variables carry directory paths across the exec boundary into CLI processes. These are derived from BURC configuration and exported by bud_dispatch.sh during zbud_setup():
208	
209	| Variable | Source | Value |
210	|----------|--------|-------|
211	| `BURD_TOOLS_DIR` | `BURC_TOOLS_DIR` | Project tools root directory |
212	| `BURD_BUK_DIR` | Derived from `BURC_TOOLS_DIR` | BUK subdirectory (`$BURC_TOOLS_DIR/buk`) |
213	| `BURD_TABTARGET_DIR` | `BURC_TABTARGET_DIR` | Tabtarget directory |
214	
215	These variables are available in exec'd CLI processes without requiring the CLI to re-source BURC. CLIs that need stable paths to tools or utilities should use these variables rather than reconstructing paths from `BASH_SOURCE`.
216	
217	### Template 2: CLI Entry Point
218	
219	BUD variables are provided by dispatch. The CLI header sources only `buc_command.sh`; all other dependencies are sourced inside the furnish function. This enables doc-mode help display without runtime env vars — furnish gates after `buc_doc_env` calls before any sourcing that might fail.
220	
221	```bash
222	# «shebang»
223	# «copyright»
224	
225	set -euo pipefail
226	
227	source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buc_command.sh"
228	
229	z«prefix»_furnish() {
230	
231	  # Document only the BUD variables actually used
232	  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
233	  buc_doc_env "BURD_TOOLS_DIR        " "Project tools root directory (dispatch-provided)"
234	  buc_doc_env "«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE " "Module specific configuration file"
235	  buc_doc_env_done || return 0
236	
237	  # Source all dependencies
238	  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh"
239	  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/«prefix»_«name».sh"
240	
241	  # Source regime files, if any
242	  source "${«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source regime file"
243	
244	  z«prefix»_kindle
245	  z«prefix»_enforce  # Regime archetype: ironclad gate after kindle (omit if no regime)
246	}
247	
248	buc_execute «prefix»_ "«module_description_phrase»" z«prefix»_furnish "$@"
249	
250	# eof
251	```
252	
253	### Differential Furnish (conditional dependency sourcing)
254	
255	The furnish function receives the **command name** as `$1`, enabling conditional sourcing based on which command will execute. This allows heavy dependencies (e.g., GCP client libraries, OAuth flows) to be loaded only for commands that need them.
256	
257	**Pattern**: Light dependencies always loaded; heavy dependencies conditionally sourced per command.
258	
259	```bash
260	z«prefix»_furnish() {
261	  local z_command="${1:-}"
262	
263	  # Document environment variables
264	  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
265	  buc_doc_env "BURD_TOOLS_DIR        " "Project tools root directory (dispatch-provided)"
266	  buc_doc_env "«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE " "Module configuration file"
267	  buc_doc_env_done || return 0
268	
269	  # Always source light dependencies
270	  source "${«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source regime"
271	  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh" || buc_die "Failed to source validation"
272	
273	  # Conditionally source heavy dependencies based on command
274	  case "${z_command}" in
275	    «prefix»_survey|«prefix»_audit)
276	      # These commands need GCP and OAuth infrastructure
277	      source "${BURD_TOOLS_DIR}/some/heavy_dep.sh" || buc_die "Failed to source heavy deps"
278	      zheavy_kindle
279	      ;;
280	    «prefix»_validate|«prefix»_render)
281	      # These commands work with config only
282	      ;;
283	  esac
284	
285	  z«prefix»_kindle
286	}
287	```
288	
289	**Key points:**
290	- The furnish function receives the command name (e.g., "«prefix»_validate") as `$1`
291	- Existing furnish functions that don't use `$1` are unaffected (extra arg ignored in bash)
292	- Light, always-needed dependencies are sourced unconditionally
293	- Heavy dependencies are sourced only for the commands that need them (avoiding startup overhead)
294	- The `case` statement can be omitted if a module has no conditional dependencies
295	
296	**Benefits:**
297	- Faster startup for lightweight commands (validate, render, list)
298	- Heavy infrastructure (GCP SDKs, OAuth flows) loaded only when actually needed
299	- Reduced memory footprint for simple operations
300	- Clear visibility of which commands depend on what infrastructure
301	
302	### Template 3: Implementation Module
303	
304	```bash
305	# «shebang»
306	# «copyright»
307	
308	set -euo pipefail
309	
310	# Multiple inclusion detection
311	test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
312	Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED=1
313	
314	# Tinder constants (pure string literals or tinder-on-tinder composition — available at source time)
315	# «PREFIX»_some_path="fixed-filename.env"
316	# «PREFIX»_some_prefix="prefix_"
317	
318	######################################################################
319	# Internal Functions (z«prefix»_*)
320	
321	z«prefix»_kindle() {
322	  test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» already kindled"
323	
324	  # Validate only the BURD variables actually used
325	  buv_dir_exists "${BURD_TEMP_DIR}"   # If using temp files
326	  buv_dir_exists "${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}"  # If producing outputs
327	  test -n "${BURD_NOW_STAMP:-}" || buc_die "BURD_NOW_STAMP is unset"  # If using timestamps
328	
329	  # Validate module specific environment
330	  buv_file_exists "${«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE}"  # If present
331	
332	  # Source credentials into environment only - never write to disk
333	  source "${REGIME_CRED_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source credentials"
334	
335	  # Kindle constant (public — safe to read from other modules; never a secret)
336	  «PREFIX»_PUBLIC_SELECTED_NAME="some-specific-name"
337	
338	  # Kindle constants (internal)
339	  Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_temp.txt"  # Non-secret data only
340	  Z«PREFIX»_RESULT_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_«step_number»_result.json"
341	  Z«PREFIX»_CONFIG_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_«step_number»_config_"     # rest of file name appended on use
342	  Z«PREFIX»_MANIFEST_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_«step_number»_manifest_"   # rest of file name appended on use
343	
344	  # Temporary files (if using BURD_OUTPUT_DIR)
345	  Z«PREFIX»_OUTPUT_RESULT="${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/result.txt"
346	
347	  Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
348	}
349	
350	z«prefix»_sentinel() {
351	  test "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module «prefix» not kindled - call z«prefix»_kindle first"
352	}
353	
354	# Helper functions follow
355	
356	######################################################################
357	# External Functions («prefix»_*)
358	
359	«prefix»_«command»() {
360	  z«prefix»_sentinel
361	
362	  local z_param1="${1:-}"
363	  local z_param2="${2:-}"
364	
365	  # Documentation block
366	  buc_doc_brief "«one_line_description»"
367	  buc_doc_param "«required_arg»" "«one_line_description»"
368	  buc_doc_oparm "«optional_arg»" "«one_line_description»"
369	  buc_doc_shown || return 0
370	
371	  # Implementation
372	  test -n "${z_param1}" || buc_die "Parameter 'param1' is required"
373	
374	  buc_step "«user_visible_milestone»"
375	  buc_log_args "«detailed_progress_information»"
376	  [command] || buc_die "«failure_explanation»"
377	
378	  buc_success "«success_message»"
379	}
380	```
381	
382	**Kindle constant — anti-pattern:**
383	
384	```bash
385	# ❌ Kindle constant defined outside kindle
386	z«prefix»_setup() {
387	  Z«PREFIX»_CACHED_PATH="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/cache"   # VIOLATION: must be in kindle
388	}
389	
390	# ✅ Kindle constants defined in kindle, used elsewhere
391	z«prefix»_kindle() {
392	  Z«PREFIX»_CACHED_PATH="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/cache"
393	  Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
394	}
395	```
396	
397	**Tinder vs kindle constant — choosing correctly:**
398	
399	```bash
400	# ✅ Tinder constant: pure string, available at source time
401	«PREFIX»_config_file="config.env"        # lower_snake name — no kindle needed
402	
403	# ✅ Tinder-on-tinder composition: expansion of earlier tinder constant
404	«PREFIX»_role_director="director"
405	«PREFIX»_fact_ext_director="ext-${«PREFIX»_role_director}"  # resolves at source time
406	
407	# ✅ Kindle constant: depends on runtime state, requires kindle
408	«PREFIX»_CONFIG_DIR="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/config"  # SCREAMING name — kindle required
409	
410	# ❌ Wrong: runtime variable expansion in tinder position
411	«PREFIX»_config_dir="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/config"  # VIOLATION: BURD_TEMP_DIR is runtime — must be kindle
412	
413	# ❌ Wrong: computation in tinder position
414	«PREFIX»_color_red=$(z«prefix»_color '1;31')   # VIOLATION: $() — must be in kindle
415	
416	# ❌ Wrong: expansion of kindle constant in tinder position
417	«PREFIX»_derived="${«PREFIX»_KINDLED_VAR}/sub"  # VIOLATION: KINDLED_VAR available only after kindle
418	```
419	
420	## Variable Handling (General Rules)
421	
422	### Expansion Requirements
423	
424	**Always use braced, quoted expansion:**
425	```bash
426	"${z_var}"              # Required pattern - prevents word splitting and glob expansion
427	"${Z_MODULE_VAR}"       # Module variables from kindle
428	"${1:-}"                # Parameters with defaults
429	```
430	
431	**Never use:**
432	```bash
433	$var                    # Unquoted - word splitting risk
434	"$var"                  # Unbraced - boundary ambiguity
435	${var}                  # Unquoted - glob expansion risk
436	```
437	
438	**No tilde in quoted strings** — tilde expansion is suppressed inside quotes:
439	```bash
440	# ❌ Tilde does not expand in quotes
441	"~/Downloads/file.txt"          # Literal ~ character, not home directory
442	
443	# ✅ Use $HOME instead
444	"${HOME}/Downloads/file.txt"    # Expands correctly
445	```
446	
447	**Quote inside parameter expansion operators:**
448	```bash
449	# ✅ Inner expansion quoted separately — matches literally
450	"${z_path%"${z_suffix}"}"       # Strip suffix
451	"${z_path#"${z_prefix}"}"       # Strip prefix
452	
453	# ❌ Inner expansion unquoted — matches as a glob pattern
454	"${z_path%${z_suffix}}"         # z_suffix with * or ? chars matches as glob!
455	"${z_path#${z_prefix}}"         # Same risk
456	```
457	
458	When using `%`, `%%`, `#`, `##` operators with a variable as the pattern, the inner expansion must be quoted separately. Without inner quotes, the pattern is glob-expanded — a suffix containing `*` or `?` silently matches more than intended.
459	
460	### Assignment Patterns
461	
462	```bash
463	# Direct assignment
464	local z_message="Processing item"
465	
466	# ❌ NEVER declare multiple variables on one line
467	local z_name z_value z_result  # Hidden initialization failures!
468	
469	# ✅ ALWAYS one declaration per line with explicit initialization
470	local z_name=""
471	local z_value="default"
472	local z_result=0
473	
474	# ❌ NEVER use local -i — silently coerces non-integer values to 0
475	local -i z_count="${z_input}"  # "abc" becomes 0 — violates no-silent-failures!
476	
477	# ✅ Use plain local with explicit validation
478	local z_count="${z_input}"
479	test "${z_count}" -ge 0 2>/dev/null || buc_die "z_count must be non-negative integer, got: ${z_count}"
480	
481	# ✅ Two-line pattern for captured values (explicit and debuggable)
482	local z_token
483	z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture token"
484	
485	# ❌ NEVER combine declaration with capture
486	local z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture)  # Hides exit status!
487	
488	# From file using builtin $(<file) - NOT command substitution, a bash builtin
489	local z_content=$(<"${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_FILE}")
490	test -n "${z_content}" || buc_die "Failed to read or empty: ${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_FILE}"

<!--
